author: Foucault
title: The Order of Things
description: Knowledge organized by discontinuous epistemes. 'Man' is a recent invention, perhaps nearing its end.

author: Daston & Park
title: Wonders and the Order of Nature
description: Wonder was a legitimate cognitive stance for centuries before Enlightenment rationalism expelled it from natural philosophy.

author: Park
title: Secrets of Women
description: Dissection emerged from women's religious and familial practices, not university medicine. The uterus became the emblem of hidden knowledge.

author: Merchant
title: The Death of Nature
description: The Scientific Revolution replaced an organic, feminized nature with a mechanical worldview that licensed exploitation and extraction.

author: Smith
title: The Body of the Artisan
description: Artisans possessed tacit, bodily knowledge — 'knowledge produced through bodily labor' — that constituted genuine scientific expertise.

author: Gómez
title: The Experiential Caribbean
description: Black Caribbean Mohanes led their own epistemological revolution through experiential, sensory knowledge outside European frameworks.

author: Shapin
title: The Scientific Revolution
description: 'There was no such thing as the scientific revolution.' Science was always situated, contingent, messy, and politically fraught.

author: Bleichmar
title: Visible Empire
description: Spanish botanical expeditions were visualization projects. Illustrations were the science, not supplements to it.

author: Burke
title: What Is the History of Knowledge?
description: 'Science' is too narrow; tactical knowledge, bureaucratic knowledge, Indigenous knowledge, bodily knowledge all deserve systematic study.

author: Biagioli
title: Galileo, Courtier
description: Galileo's courtly role didn't just fund but directly shaped his science. Disputes were 'processes of self-fashioning.'

author: Shapin & Schaffer
title: Leviathan and the Air-Pump
description: 'Solutions to the problem of knowledge are solutions to the problem of social order.' Three technologies of fact-making: material, literary, social.

author: Latour
title: We Have Never Been Modern
description: The Nature/Society divide is illusory. All knowledge consists of quasi-objects. 'The less the moderns think they are blended, the more they blend.'

author: Daston & Galison
title: Objectivity
description: Successive regimes of objectivity — truth-to-nature, mechanical, trained judgment — each demanded a different kind of scientific self.

author: Chang
title: Inventing Temperature
description: Even 'boiling point = 100°C' rests on circular reasoning. Science advances by epistemic iteration, not foundational certainty.

author: Golinski
title: The Experimental Self
description: Humphry Davy self-fashioned into a scientist by inhabiting overlapping personae — enthusiast, experimentalist, philosopher, traveller.

author: Porter
title: Trust in Numbers
description: Quantification is a 'technology of distance' — adopted not by powerful experts but by weak, divided ones under external scrutiny.

author: Warwick
title: Masters of Theory
description: Cambridge's Mathematical Tripos physically shaped problem-solving habits. You can trace a physicist's training in how they solve problems.

author: Secord
title: Victorian Sensation
description: The 'Darwinian revolution' was as much about reading publics and how ideas circulated as about evolutionary theory per se.

author: Nye
title: Before Big Science
description: Physics and chemistry developed distinct identities from the same natural philosophy traditions. The 'atom' meant different things to each.

author: Nyhart
title: Modern Nature
description: German animal ecology emerged from civic museums, schools, and teachers — not universities. Möbius coined 'biocoenosis' studying oyster beds.

author: Hopwood
title: Haeckel's Embryos
description: Scientific images have material histories. Haeckel's grids were continuously remade, not 'mindlessly recycled.' Fraud is socially constructed.

author: Hennessy
title: On the Backs of Tortoises
description: The Galápagos were never pristine 'evolutionary Eden' — always contested among tortoises, sailors, colonists, scientists, tourists.

author: Rogaski
title: Knowing Manchuria
description: Sensory and environmental knowledge on an Asian borderland, shaped by competing imperial projects.

author: Franklin
title: Dolly Mixtures
description: Cloning situated in the longue durée of sheep breeding and pastoral capitalism. 'Stock' = biology, capital, lineage simultaneously.

author: Kevles
title: The Physicists
description: American physics driven by desire for autonomy and resources. Tension between 'best science elitism' and democratic accountability.

author: Gordin
title: Scientific Babel
description: English rose to dominance through geopolitical accident, not intellectual superiority. Translation always transforms scientific content.

author: Kohler
title: Lords of the Fly
description: Fruit flies were mass-produced technologies with a moral economy of exchange. The fly room as a large sociotechnical system.

author: Haraway
title: Primate Visions
description: Primatology is simultaneously science, spectacle, and myth. Apes are stand-ins for gender, race, and imperial power.

author: Galison
title: Image & Logic
description: Physics = semi-autonomous traditions (image, logic, theory) at different tempos, communicating through 'trading zones.' Against Kuhn.

author: Lovejoy
title: Tales of Militant Chemistry
description: Kodak's film chemistry → poison gas → atomic bomb. Agfa used concentration camp labor. Industrial chemistry and war are inseparable.

author: Beller
title: Quantum Dialogue
description: Kuhn's paradigm model is circular — derived from Copenhagen physics, then used to validate it. Creative disagreement ('dialogism') drives science.

author: Healey
title: The Gulag Doctors
description: Medical knowledge produced under coercion. Healthcare workers held dual loyalties: to medicine and to the Gulag system.

author: Mitman
title: Empire of Rubber
description: Firestone in Liberia: corporate slave trade under the banner of 'development.' Globalization replicated Atlantic extraction.

author: Latour & Woolgar
title: Laboratory Life
description: Facts become solid through accumulated investments making alternatives costly. 'The result of construction is that it appears unconstructed.'

author: Collins
title: Changing Order
description: The 'experimenter's regress': replication cannot resolve disputes. Social negotiation does, then gets washed away in the final account.

author: Pickering
title: Constructing Quarks
description: 'Opportunism' — the right mix of social milieu, available information, and rhetoric — determines which theories gain traction.

author: Milam
title: Creatures of Cain
description: Cold War anxieties about human nature played out through animal behavior research. 'Colloquial science' as a space where ideas are truly contested.

author: Serlin
title: Replaceable You
description: Postwar prosthetics and gender-affirming surgery as allegories for national rehabilitation and contested masculinity/femininity.

author: Rheinberger
title: Toward a History of Epistemic Things
description: The 'experimental system' — a hybrid of epistemic and technical things — generates unprecedented events, not planned discoveries.

author: Creager
title: Life Atomic
description: Radioisotopes from Oak Ridge crossed civilian-military boundaries into biology, ecology, medicine. Classic dual-use technology.

author: Kaiser
title: Drawing Theories Apart
description: Feynman diagrams 'adapted as adopted.' Theoretical physics is craft practice; you can trace training through paper-tool usage.

author: Krige
title: American Hegemony
description: US scientific aid to Europe was consensual hegemony — coproduced informal empire through grants, fellowships, and institutional models.

author: Erickson et al.
title: Cold War Rationality
description: The interdisciplinary debate: formal rationality vs. deliberative reason. From the Berlin Airlift to Kahneman & Tversky.

author: Light
title: From Warfare to Welfare
description: Defense intellectuals transferred military techniques to city planning. Technologies failed, but the technocratic power elite persisted.

author: Jasanoff (ed.)
title: States of Knowledge
description: Co-production: ways of knowing the world are inseparable from ways of choosing to live in it.

author: Medina
title: Cybernetic Revolutionaries
description: Chile's Cybersyn: political values live in organizational arrangements, not in the machines themselves.

author: Hashmi
title: Making Reliable Persons
description: Pakistan's biometric database reinforces genealogical logics rather than replacing them. The computer formalizes what was already present.

author: Rabinow
title: Making PCR
description: PCR invented at Cetus Corporation — frontier science in a corporate environment, rare in the historiography.
